FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, baseline 2016-2018, N=10,317 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyberbullying victimization as outcome, not smartphone/social media as exposure. Cyberbullying alone without separately measured phone/SM variable fails FT-IC2 per E6.

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical longitudinal analysis from ABCD baseline to Year 2, logistic regression models

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 not applicable - paper fails FT-IC2 and does not measure smartphone/SM as primary exposure variable
notes: EXCLUDED: This paper studies ACEs predicting cyberbullying victimization, but does not measure smartphone or social media use as an exposure variable. Cyberbullying victimization is the outcome, not smartphone/SM time/use. Fails FT-IC2 screening.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9,443 early adolescents ages 10-14

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyberbullying (victimization and perpetration) as exposure, not smartphone/social media. No smartphone time, social media use, or app usage measured. Cyberbullying alone without separately measured phone/SM fails E6.

FT_IC5.evidence: Empirical cross-sectional analysis, Modified Poisson regression examining cyberbullying associations with sleep disturbance

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 not applicable - paper fails FT-IC2, does not measure smartphone/SM as exposure
notes: EXCLUDED: Paper studies cyberbullying (victimization and perpetration) as predictor of sleep disturbance, but does not measure smartphone or social media use. No phone/SM exposure variable. Fails FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, Year 2 (2018-2020), N=10,339 early adolescents ages 10-14

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple smartphone and social media variables: recreational screen time, problematic use of video games, social media, and mobile phones. Specific exposures include texting, social media, video chat, YouTube, and internet browsing.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses estimating associations between sexual orientation (predictor) and recreational screen time, problematic screen use (outcomes). Multiple tests reported with coefficients.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: sexual minority vs heterosexual = 3.72 more hours daily screen time (95% CI 2.96-4.47); possible sexual minority = 1.58 more hours (95% CI 0.92-2.24). Coefficients for problematic use reported.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis from ABCD data, multiple linear regression models

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (not longitudinal), and paper reports smartphone/SM as outcome with individual predictor (sexual orientation/identity), which is not demographic-only. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: INCLUDE: Meets all inclusion criteria. Uses ABCD, measures multiple smartphone/SM variables (screen time, social media, texting, video chat), reports multiple regression associations with sexual orientation, provides numeric coefficients with CIs. Cross-sectional analysis excludes FT-EC7.
